text: Here we present a case of a patient suffering from superior mesenteric artery syndrome managed with a robotic strong procedure. Our patient was a twenty-six year old female who had a several year history of nausea, vomiting, postprandial abdominal pain, and flushing associated with an eighty pound weight loss. She had previously been managed with a PEG-J tube and initially improved with gain of weight. However, her weight loss and symptoms recurred after the PEG-J was removed.

She had a CTA and upper GI series which were consistent with superior mesenteric artery syndrome. On upper GI imaging, we can see a focal narrowing of the third portion of the duodenum with diminished transit of contrast. During dynamic imaging, antiparastalsis against the narrowing was also demonstrated. CTA demonstrated an aorto-SMA angle of approximately fourteen degrees, more acute than the expected angle of twenty-five to sixty degrees, and consistent with the diagnosis of superior mesenteric artery syndrome.

The surgical team demonstrates the port placement for the robotic procedure, marking all robotic ports with an 'R'. The third robotic arm, used as the camera, is labeled with a 'C'. Two assistant ports, labeled 'A-one' and 'A-two', are also utilized.

The surgeon begins the dissection by mobilizing the ligament of Treitz from the left side. Cotillier forceps are used in robotic arms one and two, while hook electrocautery is employed in robotic arm four to dissect the peritoneum overlying the proximal jejunum. The bowel is retracted inferiorly using atraumatic graspers to facilitate the dissection.

The surgeon preserves the inferior mesenteric vein, retracting it laterally and cephalad to prevent injury during dissection. The aorta is visible to the left of the proximal jejunum. The dissection continues as the thin peritoneal attachments of the bowel are divided beneath the superior mesenteric artery (SMA).

The surgeon uses hook electrocautery to take down adhesions from a previous laparoscopic cholecystectomy. The lateral wall of the duodenum is identified, and a combination of electrocautery and blunt dissection is employed to separate the duodenum from the transverse colon mesentery.
The surgeon medializes the duodenum, revealing the inferior vena cava. The dissection plane during the COCR maneuver is carefully maintained along the duodenum to prevent injury.

The surgeon mobilizes the third portion of the duodenum, proceeding cephalad along the anterior surface of the inferior vena cava. The duodenum is retracted medially to facilitate the removal of retroperitoneal attachments at the ligament of trites. A window is created posterior to the duodenum, revealing the aorta visible from the right side of the ligament of trites.

The surgeon completes the takedown of the ligament of trites and delivers the proximal jejunum through the retroperitoneum using Caudillere forceps and an assistant grasper. Electrocautery is then used to enlarge the retroperitoneal window by dissecting back towards the patient's left.

The surgeon mobilizes a vascularized omental flap using a ligature to divide the omentum. The omentum is then secured at the apex of the acute angle between the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) and the aorta, preventing the bowel from slipping back.

The patient was discharged from the hospital on postoperative day two, tolerating a regular diet with complete resolution of her preoperative symptoms. She is now home, no longer having postprandial nausea and vomiting. Postoperative upper GI imaging clearly shows the duodenum and mobilized jejunum within the right upper quadrant, as well as contrast flowing freely to the rest of the small bowel.
We believe that in appropriately selected patients, the STRONG procedure is an appropriate operation for symptomatic relief and that it can be done robotically in the hands of a skilled surgeon.
